content:
Target split: four services aligned to domains.

Orders: owns order lifecycle, order_items, order_state. Source of truth for customer intent.
Billing: owns payments, invoices, refunds, payment_methods. Owns Stripe/PSP integration.
Fulfillment: owns shipments, warehouse handoff, tracking, returns intake.
Inventory: owns stock_levels, reservations, SKU catalog read model.

Each service: own Postgres schema, own deploy pipeline, own Kafka topics. No cross-service DB reads. No shared tables.

Order-to-payment strong consistency without 2PC: use orchestrated saga inside Orders service.
Orders writes order in `pending_payment` state in own DB.
Orders calls Billing synchronously over gRPC: `AuthorizePayment(order_id, amount, idempotency_key)`.
Billing writes payment row, calls PSP, returns result in same request.
On success Orders transitions to `confirmed`, emits `OrderConfirmed` event.
On failure Orders transitions to `payment_failed`, emits `OrderFailed`.
Idempotency key = order_id + attempt_number, stored in Billing payment table unique index.
Timeout on Billing call = compensating action: Orders marks `payment_unknown`, reconciler polls Billing every 30s until resolved.

Why sync here: customer waiting at checkout, need answer in <2s, two-hop saga keeps blast radius small, no third party in critical path.

Everything else async via Kafka.
`OrderConfirmed` consumed by Inventory (decrement reservation to commit) and Fulfillment (create shipment).
`ShipmentCreated`, `ShipmentDispatched` consumed by Orders (state projection) and Billing (capture authorized payment on dispatch).
`InventoryReserved`, `InventoryReleased` consumed by Orders (gate confirmation).
`RefundIssued` consumed by Orders, Inventory (restock).

Reservation flow: Orders calls Inventory sync `Reserve(sku, qty, order_id, ttl=15min)` before payment. Inventory holds soft reservation in own DB. On `OrderConfirmed` reservation commits. On TTL expiry or `OrderFailed` reservation released. Prevents oversell without distributed lock.

Data ownership rules.
One writer per entity. Other services hold read projections built from Kafka events, never queried across service boundary at request time.
Cross-service joins forbidden. Denormalize into local read models.
Customer + SKU master data: separate small read-only catalog service, replicated via CDC into each consumer.
Event schemas in shared Avro registry, backwards-compatible evolution required.
Outbox pattern in every service: write entity + event to same DB transaction, relay to Kafka via Debezium. Guarantees event emission without dual-write.

Synchronous protocols.
gRPC for internal service-to-service request/response (Orders↔Billing, Orders↔Inventory).
REST/GraphQL at edge gateway only.
Sync calls allowed only inside checkout saga, max 2 hops, hard deadline 800ms per hop.
All other inter-service communication async via Kafka.

Async protocols.
Kafka topics per aggregate type, partitioned by entity id (order_id, sku_id) for ordering.
At-least-once delivery, consumers idempotent via event_id dedup table.
Dead letter topic per consumer, alerted on depth.

Test suite split: each service own suite, target <3min. Contract tests (Pact) at service boundaries replace cross-service integration tests in CI.

Migration sequence over 6 months.
Month 1: extract Inventory first. Lowest coupling, clear boundary, no checkout dependency on day one. Build outbox + Kafka topics. Run dual-write shadow mode.
Month 2: extract Fulfillment. Consumes events only, no sync caller of monolith.
Month 3: extract Billing. Hardest sync contract. Build gRPC Orders→Billing inside monolith first as in-process call, then flip to network call.
Month 4: extract Orders. By now monolith is mostly Orders code. Cutover via traffic shift on order_id hash.
Month 5: decommission monolith DB tables, remove dual-write.
Month 6: harden — chaos tests on saga, reservation TTL tuning, contract test coverage gate.

Migrations stop blocking deploys: each service migrates own schema independently, expand-contract pattern (add column, dual-write, backfill, switch read, drop old) avoids release coupling.

Team allocation: 2 eng per service during extraction, rotate so all 8 know all 4 services by month 6. Avoids knowledge silos with team of 8.

Risks.
Saga compensation logic is the hardest correctness surface — invest in property tests for state machine.
Kafka becomes critical infra — need mirror cluster + consumer lag SLOs before cutover.
Read model staleness will surprise product — set explicit eventual-consistency SLA (target p99 <500ms event-to-projection) and surface in UI where it matters.

Orders service.
Owns exclusively: orders, order_items, order_state_history, order_addresses (snapshot at checkout), saga_state, outbox_orders.
Reads from others: customer profile (id, email, default_address) from Customers via Kafka projection table `customers_read` (CDC stream). Payment status from Billing via `payments_read` projection (event-driven). Shipment status from Fulfillment via `shipments_read` projection. Inventory reservation result sync via gRPC `Inventory.Reserve` during checkout, then async `InventoryReserved`/`InventoryReleased` events for state.
Publishes: OrderCreated, OrderConfirmed, OrderCancelled, OrderFailed, OrderItemAdded, OrderShippedProjection (internal), OrderRefundRequested.

Billing service.
Owns exclusively: payments, payment_attempts, payment_methods, invoices, invoice_lines, refunds, psp_webhook_log, idempotency_keys, outbox_billing.
Reads from others: order header (order_id, total, currency, customer_id) via sync gRPC `AuthorizePayment` request payload — no DB read of orders table. Customer billing address from Customers projection `customers_read`. Shipment dispatch event from Fulfillment via Kafka to trigger capture.
Publishes: PaymentAuthorized, PaymentCaptured, PaymentFailed, PaymentRefunded, InvoiceIssued, ChargebackReceived.

Fulfillment service.
Owns exclusively: shipments, shipment_items, shipment_events (carrier scans), warehouse_assignments, returns, return_items, outbox_fulfillment.
Reads from others: order line items + ship-to address via OrderConfirmed event payload (full denormalized snapshot, no callback). SKU dimensions/weight from Inventory projection `skus_read`. Customer contact from Customers projection `customers_read`.
Publishes: ShipmentCreated, ShipmentDispatched, ShipmentDelivered, ShipmentFailed, ReturnReceived, ReturnRestocked.

Inventory service.
Owns exclusively: stock_levels, stock_movements, inventory_holds (reservations), skus, sku_attributes, warehouse_locations, outbox_inventory.
Reads from others: nothing at request time. Consumes OrderConfirmed (commit hold), OrderCancelled/OrderFailed (release hold), ReturnRestocked (increment stock), ShipmentDispatched (decrement on-hand from reserved).
Publishes: InventoryReserved, InventoryReservationFailed, InventoryReleased, InventoryCommitted, StockLevelChanged, SkuCreated, SkuUpdated.

Customers service (extracted as separate small service, single owner of identity).
Owns exclusively: customers, customer_addresses, customer_consents, auth_credentials (or delegated to identity provider), outbox_customers.
Reads from others: none.
Publishes: CustomerCreated, CustomerUpdated, AddressAdded, AddressUpdated, ConsentChanged. Streamed via CDC + Kafka into every other service's `customers_read` projection.

Sync protocol matrix.
Orders → Inventory: gRPC Reserve, Release (checkout path, <300ms deadline).
Orders → Billing: gRPC AuthorizePayment (checkout path, <800ms deadline).
Edge gateway → Orders: REST/GraphQL.
No other sync calls allowed. All other cross-service data flows via Kafka projections.

Async protocol.
All `*_read` projection tables hydrated by dedicated consumer per service, keyed by source aggregate id, idempotent via event_id dedup.
All publishers use outbox + Debezium → Kafka, partitioned by aggregate id (order_id, payment_id, shipment_id, sku_id, customer_id) to preserve per-entity ordering.

Ownership invariants.
Every table above has exactly one writer service.
Foreign keys never cross service DB. Cross-service references stored as opaque ids (order_id UUID in payments, customer_id UUID in orders) — no FK constraint, validated at write time via projection lookup or trusted event payload.
Joins across domains happen only in read models, never at request time across services.
Schema changes to owned tables are local; schema changes to event contracts go through Avro registry compatibility check.

Edge cases resolved.
Order total: computed by Orders at checkout, frozen on order row, passed to Billing in AuthorizePayment call. Billing never recomputes from order_items.
Address at time of order: snapshotted into order_addresses (Orders owns) — not a live FK to customer_addresses. Customer can change address later without mutating historical order.
SKU price: snapshotted into order_items at checkout. Inventory owns current price on skus, Orders owns historical price on order_items.
Refund initiation: Orders publishes OrderRefundRequested; Billing consumes, executes refund, publishes PaymentRefunded; Inventory consumes via ReturnRestocked from Fulfillment (returns flow) — refund money path and restock path decoupled.

Saga owner: Orders service. Orchestrated saga (not choreographed) — single state machine in Orders drives transitions, calls collaborators, records every step in saga_state table.

States.
DRAFT — cart materialized, not submitted.
PENDING_RESERVATION — submit clicked, reservation call in flight.
RESERVED — inventory_holds confirmed, awaiting payment authorization.
PENDING_PAYMENT — AuthorizePayment call in flight.
PAYMENT_UNKNOWN — Billing call timed out, outcome ambiguous.
AUTHORIZED — payment authorized, awaiting fulfillment trigger.
CONFIRMED — OrderConfirmed emitted, fulfillment + capture downstream.
CAPTURED — payment captured on dispatch.
PARTIALLY_FULFILLED — some shipments dispatched, others failed.
FULFILLED — all shipments dispatched.
CANCELLING — compensation in progress.
CANCELLED — terminal, all compensations done.
FAILED — terminal, unrecoverable error after exhausted retries, manual intervention.

Transitions and steps.

DRAFT → PENDING_RESERVATION on SubmitOrder command.
Step: write order row, write saga_state row with saga_id = order_id, idempotency_key = order_id, attempt = 1.

PENDING_RESERVATION → RESERVED on Inventory.Reserve success.
Step: gRPC Reserve(order_id, items, ttl=15min). Idempotency: Inventory dedup by order_id; repeated calls return same hold_id.
On Reserve fail (out of stock) → CANCELLED, emit OrderFailed(reason=oos). No compensation needed (nothing reserved).

RESERVED → PENDING_PAYMENT on internal tick.
Step: call Billing.AuthorizePayment(order_id, amount, idempotency_key=order_id+":auth:1").

PENDING_PAYMENT → AUTHORIZED on Billing OK.
PENDING_PAYMENT → PAYMENT_UNKNOWN on timeout (>800ms or HTTP timeout).
PENDING_PAYMENT → CANCELLING on Billing definitive decline.

PAYMENT_UNKNOWN handling.
Reconciler job polls Billing.GetPaymentStatus(idempotency_key) every 30s, max 30min.
Resolve to AUTHORIZED or CANCELLING based on Billing truth.
Billing is single source of truth for payment state — Orders never assumes.
If unresolved after 30min → FAILED, page on-call.

AUTHORIZED → CONFIRMED on internal tick.
Step: emit OrderConfirmed event via outbox (atomic with state write). Inventory consumes → commits hold to stock_movements. Fulfillment consumes → creates shipment(s).

CONFIRMED → CAPTURED on PaymentCaptured event consumed.
Capture triggered by Billing on first ShipmentDispatched event consumed by Billing.

CONFIRMED → PARTIALLY_FULFILLED on ShipmentFailed event for subset.
CONFIRMED → FULFILLED on all ShipmentDispatched received.

PARTIALLY_FULFILLED handling.
For failed shipments: emit OrderRefundRequested(line_ids, partial_amount). Billing executes partial refund, publishes PaymentRefunded(partial). Inventory restocks failed lines via ReturnRestocked from Fulfillment failure handler.
Order stays in PARTIALLY_FULFILLED until refund + restock acknowledged, then transitions to FULFILLED with metadata.partial=true.

CANCELLING (compensation orchestration).
Triggered from: CANCEL command in DRAFT/RESERVED/AUTHORIZED, or definitive payment decline, or saga timeout.
Compensation steps executed in reverse causal order, each idempotent, each tracked in saga_state.steps[]:
  1. If state ≥ AUTHORIZED: call Billing.VoidAuthorization(idempotency_key=order_id+":void:1"). Billing voids if not yet captured, refunds if captured.
  2. If state ≥ RESERVED: call Inventory.Release(order_id). Idempotent — releasing already-released hold returns success.
  3. Emit OrderCancelled event.
  4. Transition to CANCELLED.
Each compensation has independent retry (exponential backoff, max 6 attempts). Failure of any compensation after retries → FAILED state, alert.

Idempotency design.
saga_state table: (saga_id, current_state, last_event_id, attempt_counters jsonb, version int).
Every state transition is conditional update: `WHERE saga_id=? AND version=?` — optimistic concurrency, no locks.
Every outbound call carries idempotency_key derived from (saga_id, step_name, attempt). Receiver dedups.
Every inbound event handler checks event_id against processed_events table inside same DB tx as state update.
Replays of same event are no-ops; missed events are picked up by reconciler scanning saga_state for stuck rows older than threshold.

Timeout handling.
Per-state deadline in saga_state.deadline_at column.
Background job scans `WHERE deadline_at < now() AND state IN (timed_out_states)` every 10s.
PENDING_RESERVATION timeout (5s) → retry once, then CANCELLING.
PENDING_PAYMENT timeout (800ms call, 5s saga) → PAYMENT_UNKNOWN (do not assume failure).
RESERVED timeout (matches Inventory TTL minus 2min buffer = 13min) → if no payment yet, CANCELLING.
AUTHORIZED timeout (5min) → emit OrderConfirmed (recover from missed tick).
CONFIRMED timeout (24h without all ShipmentDispatched) → escalate, do not auto-cancel (already charged, fulfillment investigation).

Critical failure scenarios.

Scenario A: payment timeout.
Orders in PENDING_PAYMENT, Billing call exceeds deadline.
Transition → PAYMENT_UNKNOWN immediately. Do not retry AuthorizePayment with new idempotency_key.
Reconciler calls Billing.GetPaymentStatus(idempotency_key=order_id+":auth:1") periodically.
Billing returns one of: NOT_FOUND (call never landed) → safe to retry with attempt=2; AUTHORIZED → transition AUTHORIZED; FAILED → transition CANCELLING; PENDING (PSP still working) → keep polling.
Inventory hold still valid (15min TTL covers 30min reconcile? — no, so reconciler also calls Inventory.ExtendHold(order_id, +30min) when entering PAYMENT_UNKNOWN).
No double-charge possible: idempotency_key on Billing payments table unique constraint blocks duplicate auth.

Scenario B: inventory hold released (TTL expired) after payment authorized.
Possible if AUTHORIZED transition delayed past hold TTL.
Detection: Inventory publishes InventoryReleased(order_id, reason=ttl). Orders consumer correlates with saga_state.
If state = AUTHORIZED or beyond and stock no longer available: enter CANCELLING, void payment, emit OrderFailed(reason=inventory_lost). Customer notified, refund issued.
Mitigation: Inventory.ExtendHold called by Orders on entering PENDING_PAYMENT (push TTL to +15min from auth start). Reconciler extends again on PAYMENT_UNKNOWN.
If stock reappears (rare): Orders may re-reserve and continue — but default policy is cancel + refund for predictability.

Scenario C: partial fulfillment rollback.
Multi-shipment order, one warehouse fails to dispatch.
Fulfillment publishes ShipmentFailed(shipment_id, line_ids, reason).
Orders transitions saga to PARTIALLY_FULFILLED, computes failed line subtotal.
Orders emits OrderRefundRequested(order_id, line_ids, amount=subtotal+tax_share).
Billing consumes, calls PSP refund with idempotency_key=order_id+":refund:"+line_hash, publishes PaymentRefunded.
Fulfillment publishes ShipmentReturnedToStock for failed lines (or skips if never picked).
Inventory consumes, increments stock_levels.
Orders updates order_items[failed].status=refunded, transitions to FULFILLED with partial flag once all acknowledgments received.
No 2PC: each compensation is independent idempotent message, retried until ack.

Strong consistency guarantees on order-to-payment.
Within Orders DB: order row + saga_state + outbox written in single Postgres transaction.
Within Billing DB: payment row + idempotency_key + outbox written in single Postgres transaction.
Across services: eventual consistency, but customer-visible order state never advances past payment confirmation until Billing has truthfully replied. PAYMENT_UNKNOWN is the explicit ambiguity state, never silently treated as success or failure.
No distributed lock: optimistic concurrency on saga_state.version + idempotency keys at every receiver replace need for locks.
No 2PC: saga compensations replace rollback; outbox + Kafka replace dual-write.

Operational hooks.
saga_state dashboard: count by state, age in state, stuck saga alert (>2x expected duration).
Compensation failure alert: any saga in CANCELLING with failed_compensation_attempts > 3.
PAYMENT_UNKNOWN backlog metric, page if depth > 50 or oldest > 10min.
Per-state SLO: PENDING_PAYMENT p99 < 1.5s, RESERVED→CONFIRMED p99 < 3s, full DRAFT→CONFIRMED p99 < 5s.

Window 1: order confirmation → order appears in customer order history.
Risk: customer clicks "my orders" 200ms after checkout success page, sees empty list, thinks order lost, submits again.
Cause: order history page reads from Orders read-model projection hydrated by OrderConfirmed Kafka event; producer→consumer lag.
Bound: order visible in history within 500ms p99 of confirmation.
Mitigation: read-your-writes — checkout success response includes order_id + signed token; history page does sticky read against Orders primary by order_id for 60s post-checkout (cookie hint), falling back to projection. Plus: success page itself shows order summary directly from saga response, not from history list. UI affordance: "your order #12345 is confirmed" with explicit id and link, not "check your history."

Window 2: payment authorized → invoice/receipt available.
Risk: customer wants PDF receipt immediately, Billing has authorized but invoice generation async.
Bound: receipt downloadable within 2s p99 of CONFIRMED.
Mitigation: invoice generated synchronously inside Billing AuthorizePayment handler (same DB tx as payment row), not deferred. Receipt URL returned in checkout response. If async required for tax calc, show "receipt emailed within 1 minute" affordance and disable download button with countdown rather than 404.

Window 3: order placed → inventory decrement visible to other shoppers.
Risk: two customers see "5 in stock", both add to cart, both reserve, last-to-reserve gets out-of-stock at checkout — bad but acceptable. Worse: stock_levels public read shows stale "5 in stock" for minutes after sellout, drives wasted checkouts.
Bound: stock display reflects committed stock within 1s p99, reflects reservations within 3s p99.
Mitigation: PDP (product detail page) reads `available = on_hand - active_holds` from Inventory read replica, not from cached projection in Orders/Catalog. Catalog service subscribes to StockLevelChanged + InventoryReserved/Released and updates own projection. UI affordance: show "low stock" band ("only 3 left") rather than exact count when available < 10, hides small drift. For high-velocity SKUs, push WebSocket updates from Inventory to active PDP viewers.

Window 4: shipment dispatched → tracking number visible in order detail.
Risk: customer gets carrier email with tracking before app shows it; calls support confused.
Bound: tracking visible in order detail within 2s p99 of carrier handoff.
Mitigation: Fulfillment writes tracking to own DB and emits ShipmentDispatched(tracking_number) atomically via outbox. Orders projection consumer prioritized (separate consumer group, low lag SLO). Order detail page also accepts shipment_id query and reads Fulfillment directly via API gateway as fallback for first 5min after dispatch event. UI affordance: "tracking syncing — refresh in a moment" if order shows dispatched but tracking null, instead of blank field.

Window 5: refund issued → balance reflected on customer's card statement.
Risk: this one is genuinely slow (PSP + bank, 3-10 days) and not under our control. Customer sees "refunded" in app but no money back, panics.
Bound: in-app refund status reflects Billing within 1s p99; bank reflection is out-of-scope but must be communicated.
Mitigation: order detail shows "refund issued [date] — appears on statement within 5-10 business days" verbatim. Send email at PaymentRefunded with same language. Never show "refund complete" without the bank-timing caveat.

Window 6: order cancelled → reservation released → SKU available again on PDP.
Risk: customer A cancels, SKU should reappear for customer B, but Inventory projection lag means B still sees out-of-stock.
Bound: PDP available count reflects release within 3s p99.
Mitigation: same as Window 3 (Inventory read replica + push). For limited-edition drops, accept the lag explicitly in product copy.

Window 7: address updated in customer profile → in-flight order ship-to.
Risk: customer updates address mid-checkout or shortly after order placed, expects new address used.
Bound: explicit — address is snapshotted into order_addresses at checkout submit; profile update never propagates to placed orders.
Mitigation: this is a feature not a bug, but UI must make it clear. Order detail shows "shipping to: [snapshot address]" with "edit shipping address" button that calls separate ChangeShipmentAddress command (only allowed in pre-dispatch states, routed through Fulfillment). Profile update has affordance "this won't change addresses on existing orders."

Window 8: customer profile change (name, email) → reflected across services.
Risk: customer updates email, then receives shipment notification at old email.
Bound: customer email change propagated to all consumer projections within 5s p99.
Mitigation: Customers service emits CustomerUpdated with full new payload. All consumers (Orders, Billing, Fulfillment) update local customers_read projection. Notification dispatch (e.g. shipment email) reads email from local projection at send time, not from snapshot. For email confirmations on the change itself, send to OLD email ("your email was changed to X — if not you, click here") and NEW email — covers takeover risk.

Window 9: payment captured (on dispatch) → order status shows "paid" vs "authorized".
Risk: customer in app sees "authorized — pending capture" for hours, asks why.
Bound: most users don't care about auth-vs-capture; UI should hide.
Mitigation: collapse auth + capture into single user-visible status "paid" once AUTHORIZED reached. Internal saga distinguishes; user UI does not. No window problem if not exposed.

Window 10: high-value order placed → fraud review hold → status flapping.
Risk: order shows CONFIRMED, then 30s later flips to ON_HOLD when fraud rule fires async, customer confused by reversal.
Bound: fraud decision must complete before CONFIRMED is shown to user, OR status must never regress from terminal-looking state.
Mitigation: insert FRAUD_REVIEW state between AUTHORIZED and CONFIRMED. AuthorizePayment in Billing performs synchronous fraud screen (sub-300ms rules) and returns risk_score. High-risk orders enter FRAUD_REVIEW state, UI shows "verifying — usually under 1 minute," only transitions to CONFIRMED after manual or async clear. Never show CONFIRMED then revert.

Window 11: read-after-write on saga_state from CSR/admin tools.
Risk: support agent looks up order right after customer call, sees stale state because admin tool reads projection.
Bound: support tooling reads primary, not projection.
Mitigation: admin/CSR UI reads Orders primary directly (read replica with sub-100ms lag, or primary for sub-second freshness). Customer-facing UI tolerates projection lag; internal tools do not.

Window 12: Kafka consumer lag spike (incident) → all projections stale simultaneously.
Risk: during Kafka degradation, many windows blow past SLO at once; systemic UX failure.
Bound: detect lag > 5s on any critical consumer, degrade gracefully.
Mitigation: per-projection lag metric with SLO alerts. When lag exceeds threshold, app surfaces banner "order updates may be delayed" and switches read paths for critical flows (checkout, order detail) to direct service API calls bypassing projection. Stale-while-revalidate cache with explicit "as of HH:MM:SS" timestamp on order detail during incident.

Cross-cutting principles.
Snapshot at decision time (addresses, prices, tax) — never read live for historical entities.
Read-your-writes via sticky session or explicit primary read for 30-60s after a write.
Two-tier UX: transactional success page reads from authoritative source; list/dashboard pages tolerate eventual consistency with affordances.
Never let user-visible status regress (CONFIRMED → ON_HOLD bad). Insert intermediate states instead.
Surface lag as UX state when it exceeds SLO — banner, "syncing" badge, refresh hint — better than silent staleness.
Per-consumer lag SLO published, error budget tracked, paged on burn.
Idempotent client retries: every customer-facing mutation (place order, cancel, refund request) accepts client-supplied idempotency_key so retries from flaky network never double-act.

Acceptable bounds summary.
Order in history: 500ms p99.
Receipt available: 2s p99 (or sync inside auth).
Stock display: 1s committed, 3s reservations p99.
Tracking visible: 2s p99 post-dispatch.
Refund in-app: 1s p99 (bank: 5-10 business days, communicated).
Reservation release reflected: 3s p99.
Customer profile propagation: 5s p99.
Fraud decision: synchronous in checkout, never async-revert.
Consumer lag tolerance before UX degradation banner: 5s.

We splitting our orders platform from one big application into four focused services: orders, billing, fulfillment, inventory. Today all four live in same codebase and same database, which means every team blocks every other team — releases get queued, tests take 12 minutes, database changes freeze deploys, and merge conflicts eat hours per week. After split, each team owns its service end-to-end: own code, own database, own release cadence. We keep money-critical guarantee that no customer gets charged without an order, and no order confirms without payment, by using well-known coordination pattern (saga) instead of risky distributed-transaction tech. Customer experience stays same or better; engineering velocity should roughly double once complete.

Plan runs six months. Months 1-2 extract lowest-risk services (inventory, fulfillment) to build muscle and infrastructure. Months 3-4 tackle billing and orders, the hard ones, with old and new running in parallel for safety. Months 5-6 decommission old system and harden. Main risks: Kafka (the message bus connecting services) becomes critical infrastructure and needs investment; some data will be eventually-consistent rather than instant, so a few user-facing screens need small UX tweaks (already designed); and the saga pattern has subtle edge cases around payment timeouts that demand careful testing. We have two-year runway, so timing is comfortable, and we can pause cleanly between months if priorities shift. Worst case if we abort mid-migration: we run hybrid for longer than planned, which is annoying but not dangerous.

@flint v0 hybrid
G: post_deploy_day1_health_slos
C: five_slo_budget ∧ canary_vs_control_split ∧ saga_correctness_priority ∧ kafka_critical_infra ∧ no_regression_vs_monolith
P: slo1_checkout_success_rate(">=99.5% over 5min, page if <99.0% for 10min") ∧ slo2_order_to_confirm_latency("p99 <5s, p95 <2s, page if p99 >8s for 10min") ∧ slo3_saga_stuck_count("saga_state where age_in_state > 2x_expected, page if >10 stuck or any in PAYMENT_UNKNOWN >10min") ∧ slo4_kafka_consumer_lag("per critical consumer p99 <5s, page if >30s for 5min on orders_projection or billing_capture") ∧ slo5_payment_double_charge_zero("count(payments group by idempotency_key having count>1) must equal 0, page on any") ∧ canary_query("compare canary_traffic_pct=5 vs control on checkout_success_rate, p99_latency, saga_failure_rate via prometheus rate diff over 15min sliding window, gate promotion at <0.1pp delta") ∧ dashboard_groups("row1=business_kpis, row2=saga_state_machine, row3=kafka_health, row4=service_golden_signals, row5=data_consistency_lag")
V: grafana_board("orders_split_day1") ∧ alert_routing("pagerduty_orders_oncall") ∧ canary_promote_gate("all 5 slos green for 60min before traffic_pct increment") ∧ synthetic_checkout_probe("every 60s, alert on 2 consecutive failures") ∧ rollback_runbook("flip ingress hash back to monolith, documented and rehearsed")
A: deploy_dashboard("orders_split_day1.json") ∧ wire_alerts("prometheus_rules.yaml + pagerduty_routes") ∧ enable_canary_split("5pct via order_id hash at ingress") ∧ start_synthetic_probe("checkout_e2e against canary and control") ∧ publish_runbook("day1_oncall.md with rollback steps")

[AUDIT]
Five day-1 SLOs: checkout success rate (>=99.5%), order-to-confirm latency (p99 <5s), stuck saga count (PAYMENT_UNKNOWN >10min pages), Kafka consumer lag (p99 <5s on critical projections), payment double-charge counter (must be zero). Canary at 5% traffic via order_id hash, compared against control on same three core metrics in 15min sliding Prometheus diff, promotion gate at <0.1pp delta and 60min all-green. Dashboard grouped: business KPIs, saga state machine, Kafka health, per-service golden signals, data consistency lag. Synthetic checkout probe every 60s; rollback = flip ingress hash back to monolith.

P: slo2_order_to_confirm_latency("p99 <5s, p95 <2s, page if p99 >8s for 10min")
slo3_saga_stuck_count("saga_state where age_in_state > 2x_expected, page if >10 stuck or any in PAYMENT_UNKNOWN >10min")
slo4_kafka_consumer_lag("per critical consumer p99 <5s, page if >30s for 5min on orders_projection or billing_capture")
slo5_payment_double_charge_zero("count(payments group by idempotency_key having count>1) must equal 0, page on any")
canary_query("compare canary_traffic_pct=5 vs control on checkout_success_rate, p99_latency, saga_failure_rate via prometheus rate diff over 15min sliding window, gate promotion at <0.1pp delta")
V: canary_promote_gate("all 5 slos green for 60min before traffic_pct increment")
rollback_runbook("flip ingress hash back to monolith, documented and rehearsed")
A: enable_canary_split("5pct via order_id hash at ingress")

@flint v0 hybrid
G: audit_jwt_auth_module
C: file("auth/jwt_service.py"), lib("pyjwt"), symmetric_secret_hs256, rbac
P: rank_by_severity, prioritize("auth_bypass"), fail_closed

V (severity order):
- critical_alg_none_accepted — ALGOS lists "none", unsigned tokens pass signature check
- critical_algorithm_confusion — HS256+RS256 in algorithms allowlist, attacker signs HS256 using RS256 public key as HMAC secret
- critical_exp_not_verified — options={"verify_exp": False}, expired tokens accepted forever
- critical_hardcoded_weak_secret — low-entropy SECRET in source, TODO never rotated
- high_attacker_controlled_alg — header.alg read via get_unverified_header then fed back into decode allowlist, inverts trust model
- high_no_iss_aud_kid — cross-service token replay possible
- high_no_key_rotation — single static secret, no kid lookup
- medium_role_default_user — payload.get("role","user") silently downgrades on tampered/missing claim
- medium_sub_keyerror_inconsistent — sub raises KeyError, role defaults; uneven failure modes
- medium_no_nbf_iat_jti — no replay protection, no clock skew, no revocation
- medium_no_revocation — stolen tokens valid until exp
- low_ttl_unbounded — caller-controlled ttl, no server max
- low_role_claim_trusted — no per-request server-side rbac recheck

A:
- remove "none" from ALGOS
- pin_single_algorithm("HS256") — never mixed with RS256
- drop verify_exp=False
- move_secret_to_env, 256-bit random, rotate
- ignore_header_alg — hardcode expected algorithm in decode call
- add issuer= and audience= to jwt.decode
- add kid-indexed key rotation
- reject_on_missing_claims — no defaults for sub/role
- add_jti_denylist for revocation
- enforce_server_side_rbac per request
- cap_ttl_server_side to 900s, use refresh tokens
- add nbf and iat with skew window

input.A: ["remove_none_from_algos", "pin_single_algorithm(\"algorithms=['HS256'] only, or ['RS256'] only — never mixed\")", "enable_exp_verification(\"drop options={'verify_exp': False}\")", "move_secret_to_env(\"load from env/secret manager, min 256-bit random\")", "ignore_header_alg(\"do not read get_unverified_header().alg; hardcode expected algorithm in decode call\")", "add_iss_aud_validation(\"pass issuer= and audience= to jwt.decode\")", "add_kid_key_rotation(\"support multiple keys indexed by kid for rotation\")", "reject_on_missing_claims(\"raise on missing sub/role rather than defaulting\")", "add_jti_denylist(\"issue jti, track revoked tokens\")", "enforce_server_side_rbac(\"re-check role against authoritative store per request, treat jwt role as hint only\")", "cap_ttl_server_side(\"clamp ttl to max 900s for access tokens, use refresh tokens for longer sessions\")", "add_nbf_iat(\"include nbf and iat claims, validate clock skew window\")"]

Fix: pin single algorithm, load secret from env, enable exp check, validate iss/aud, reject on missing claims, add jti denylist.

input.audit: Three critical flaws: alg=none accepted (unsigned bypass), HS256+RS256 algorithm confusion (public key as HMAC secret), verify_exp disabled. Also hardcoded weak secret, no iss/aud/kid, silent role default to user, no revocation, no rotation. Fix: pin one algorithm, env-loaded secret, enable exp, validate iss/aud, reject on missing claims, add jti denylist.

content:
Top 3 decode-path attacks, ranked by exploitability.

1. alg=none unsigned forgery — trivial, no secret needed
Header: {"alg":"none","typ":"JWT"}
Payload: {"sub":"victim","role":"admin","exp":9999999999}
Token: eyJhbGciOiJub25lIiwidHlwIjoiSldUIn0.eyJzdWIiOiJ2aWN0aW0iLCJyb2xlIjoiYWRtaW4iLCJleHAiOjk5OTk5OTk5OTl9.
Signature segment empty, "none" in ALGOS so PyJWT accepts, require_admin passes.
Why easiest: no key material needed, no crypto, just base64.

2. Expired-token replay — steal once, use forever
Any leaked/old token with exp in past still verifies because options={"verify_exp": False}.
Payload: {"sub":"victim","role":"admin","exp":1000000000} (2001)
Token signed with real HS256 secret (from breach, log leak, source repo — SECRET literal is committed).
verify() skips exp check, returns admin. No refresh, no revocation window.
Why second: requires one valid historical token but lifetime unlimited.

3. HS256/RS256 algorithm confusion — if service ever exposes RS256 public key
Attacker grabs RSA public key (JWKS, cert endpoint, GitHub).
Signs forged payload with HMAC-SHA256 using public key bytes as shared secret.
Header: {"alg":"HS256"}
Payload: {"sub":"attacker","role":"admin","exp":9999999999}
Signature: HMAC_SHA256(rsa_public_key_pem, header+"."+payload)
decode() called with algorithms=["HS256","RS256","none"] and key=SECRET — PyJWT uses SECRET as HMAC key so this path fails here; BUT combined with header.alg trust and mixed allowlist, a variant where SECRET is later swapped to the RSA public key (common during HS→RS migration) makes forgery trivial.
Why third: conditional on key material exposure or migration state, but game-over when present.

Bonus — not top 3 but worth flagging: SECRET="development-secret-do-not-commit" in source means attack 1 and 2 collapse into "attacker opens repo, signs own admin token with real HS256." Exploitability rank flips to #1 if repo ever public or leaked.

code:
```python
# auth/jwt_service.py
import os, time, logging
import jwt
from dataclasses import dataclass

log = logging.getLogger(__name__)

SECRET = os.environ["JWT_SECRET"]  # fail fast if unset
ISSUER = os.environ.get("JWT_ISS", "sigil-auth")
AUDIENCE = os.environ.get("JWT_AUD", "sigil-api")
ALGO = "HS256"
TOKEN_VERSION = 2
FIX_EPOCH = int(os.environ["JWT_FIX_EPOCH"])          # unix ts of deploy
GRANDFATHER_DEADLINE = FIX_EPOCH + 30 * 86400
MAX_TTL = 900
CLOCK_SKEW = 60

@dataclass
class Token:
    sub: str
    exp: int
    role: str

def sign(sub: str, role: str, ttl: int = MAX_TTL) -> str:
    if ttl > MAX_TTL:
        raise ValueError("ttl exceeds server max")
    now = int(time.time())
    payload = {
        "sub": sub,
        "role": role,
        "iat": now,
        "nbf": now,
        "exp": now + ttl,
        "iss": ISSUER,
        "aud": AUDIENCE,
        "ver": TOKEN_VERSION,
    }
    return jwt.encode(payload, SECRET, algorithm=ALGO)

def _decode_strict(token: str) -> dict:
    return jwt.decode(
        token, SECRET,
        algorithms=[ALGO],
        issuer=ISSUER,
        audience=AUDIENCE,
        leeway=CLOCK_SKEW,
        options={"require": ["exp", "iat", "sub", "role"]},
    )

def _decode_legacy(token: str) -> dict:
    # legacy HS256 tokens, no iss/aud/ver, but signature + exp enforced
    return jwt.decode(
        token, SECRET,
        algorithms=[ALGO],              # never "none", never RS256
        leeway=CLOCK_SKEW,
        options={"require": ["exp"]},   # exp now verified
    )

def verify(token: str) -> Token:
    now = int(time.time())
    try:
        payload = _decode_strict(token)
    except jwt.InvalidTokenError:
        if now >= GRANDFATHER_DEADLINE:
            raise
        payload = _decode_legacy(token)
        iat = payload.get("iat", 0)
        if iat and iat >= FIX_EPOCH:
            raise jwt.InvalidTokenError("post-fix token missing required claims")
        log.warning("legacy_jwt_accepted sub=%s", payload.get("sub"))
    if "sub" not in payload or "role" not in payload:
        raise jwt.InvalidTokenError("missing required claims")
    return Token(sub=payload["sub"], exp=int(payload["exp"]), role=payload["role"])

def require_admin(token_str: str) -> Token:
    t = verify(token_str)
    if t.role != "admin":
        raise PermissionError("not admin")
    return t
```

notes:
- ALGOS list removed, "none" impossible
- get_unverified_header path removed, decode pins HS256
- verify() signature preserved: (token: str) -> Token
- new tokens carry ver=2, iss, aud, iat, nbf, exp and must pass strict decode
- legacy tokens (pre-fix, no ver/iss/aud) fall through to _decode_legacy only if now < FIX_EPOCH + 30d AND iat absent or < FIX_EPOCH
- signature still verified on legacy path, exp still verified — only claim-set is relaxed
- after deadline, legacy path dead, only strict decode runs
- SECRET must be rotated out of source before deploy; JWT_FIX_EPOCH pinned at deploy time

@flint v0 hybrid
G: write_pytest_regression_tests_for_jwt_fix

C: file("tests/test_jwt_service.py")
C: target("auth/jwt_service.py")
C: lib("pyjwt"), lib("pytest")
C: env_vars_required("JWT_SECRET, JWT_FIX_EPOCH")

P: cover("alg_none_rejected")
P: cover("mismatched_algo_rejected")
P: cover("expired_token_rejected")
P: cover("forged_signature_rejected")
P: cover("admin_role_spoof_rejected")
P: one_assertion_per_test
P: use_fixtures_for_secret_and_epoch

V: tests_must_fail_on_pre_fix_code
V: tests_must_pass_on_fixed_code
V: no_network_no_filesystem

A: fixture_set_env_before_import
A: helper_mint_with_algo_and_key
A: assert_raises_specific_exception_type

code:
```python
# tests/test_jwt_service.py
import os
import time
import importlib
import pytest
import jwt as pyjwt

TEST_SECRET = "unit-test-secret-0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef"
FIX_EPOCH = 1_700_000_000  # arbitrary, deterministic

@pytest.fixture(scope="module")
def svc():
    os.environ["JWT_SECRET"] = TEST_SECRET
    os.environ["JWT_FIX_EPOCH"] = str(FIX_EPOCH)
    os.environ["JWT_ISS"] = "sigil-auth"
    os.environ["JWT_AUD"] = "sigil-api"
    import auth.jwt_service as m
    importlib.reload(m)
    return m

def _mint(payload, key=TEST_SECRET, algo="HS256", headers=None):
    return pyjwt.encode(payload, key, algorithm=algo, headers=headers)

def _base_payload(svc, **over):
    now = int(time.time())
    p = {
        "sub": "alice",
        "role": "user",
        "iat": now,
        "nbf": now,
        "exp": now + 300,
        "iss": "sigil-auth",
        "aud": "sigil-api",
        "ver": 2,
    }
    p.update(over)
    return p

def test_alg_none_rejected(svc):
    # PyJWT refuses to encode alg=none without allow_none; build manually
    import base64, json
    def b64(d): return base64.urlsafe_b64encode(d).rstrip(b"=").decode()
    header = b64(json.dumps({"alg": "none", "typ": "JWT"}).encode())
    payload = b64(json.dumps(_base_payload(svc, role="admin")).encode())
    token = f"{header}.{payload}."
    with pytest.raises(pyjwt.InvalidTokenError):
        svc.verify(token)

def test_mismatched_algo_rejected(svc):
    # RS256-signed token must be rejected; service pins HS256
    from cryptography.hazmat.primitives.asymmetric import rsa
    from cryptography.hazmat.primitives import serialization
    key = rsa.generate_private_key(public_exponent=65537, key_size=2048)
    pem = key.private_bytes(
        serialization.Encoding.PEM,
        serialization.PrivateFormat.PKCS8,
        serialization.NoEncryption(),
    )
    token = _mint(_base_payload(svc, role="admin"), key=pem, algo="RS256")
    with pytest.raises(pyjwt.InvalidTokenError):
        svc.verify(token)

def test_expired_token_rejected(svc):
    now = int(time.time())
    token = _mint(_base_payload(svc, iat=now - 3600, nbf=now - 3600, exp=now - 60))
    with pytest.raises(pyjwt.ExpiredSignatureError):
        svc.verify(token)

def test_forged_signature_rejected(svc):
    token = _mint(_base_payload(svc, role="admin"), key="wrong-secret")
    with pytest.raises(pyjwt.InvalidSignatureError):
        svc.verify(token)

def test_admin_role_spoof_rejected(svc):
    # tamper payload of a valid user token to claim admin, reuse original signature
    valid = _mint(_base_payload(svc, role="user"))
    h, p, s = valid.split(".")
    import base64, json
    raw = json.loads(base64.urlsafe_b64decode(p + "=="))
    raw["role"] = "admin"
    tampered_p = base64.urlsafe_b64encode(
        json.dumps(raw, separators=(",", ":")).encode()
    ).rstrip(b"=").decode()
    tampered = f"{h}.{tampered_p}.{s}"
    with pytest.raises(pyjwt.InvalidSignatureError):
        svc.verify(tampered)

def test_admin_role_spoof_via_require_admin(svc):
    # belt-and-suspenders: non-admin valid token must not pass require_admin
    token = _mint(_base_payload(svc, role="user"))
    with pytest.raises(PermissionError):
        svc.require_admin(token)
```

notes:
- fixture reloads module so env vars take effect at import time
- test_alg_none_rejected builds token manually because PyJWT blocks alg=none encoding by default
- test_mismatched_algo_rejected uses real RS256 key to prove algorithm pinning, not just allowlist filtering
- test_forged_signature uses wrong HMAC key; passes signature-check boundary
- test_admin_role_spoof_rejected mutates payload while reusing signature → InvalidSignatureError confirms signature covers payload
- extra require_admin test guards role-gate even when signature valid
- all tests run offline, deterministic, no sleep

To: Leadership
From: Security Engineering
Re: Critical authentication defect in session token service

Routine audit of our authentication module surfaced three critical defects in how user session tokens get validated. In plain terms, our system currently accepts tokens that carry no cryptographic signature at all, tokens signed with weaker methods than our policy permits, and tokens that have already expired. Any one of these alone would let an outside party impersonate any user, including administrators. Compounding matters, a placeholder credential was left in source code with a note to rotate it, and no rotation occurred. Exploiting these flaws requires only modest technical skill and no insider access.

Business impact spans every customer-facing surface that relies on login. An attacker can read, modify, or delete customer data, issue actions under administrator privilege, and do so while leaving normal audit trails intact because actions appear legitimate to downstream systems. We have no evidence of exploitation at this time, but our current logging would not reliably detect it. Risk therefore covers customer trust, contractual commitments to enterprise accounts with security addenda, and potential regulatory exposure depending on data categories in scope.

Urgency: deploy fix this week. Engineering has prepared remediation with 30-day grandfather window so existing valid sessions remain usable while new tokens are minted under stricter rules. Rollback plan and regression tests accompany fix. Credential rotation must run in parallel and complete before fix goes live. Delay past one week materially increases exposure given public nature of vulnerability class once remediation ships and gets reverse-engineered from release notes or commit history.

Disclosure obligations depend on two questions Legal and Compliance must answer: did exploitation occur, and do any affected customer contracts or regulatory regimes (GDPR, SOC 2, HIPAA where applicable, state breach-notification statutes) define this class of defect as reportable absent confirmed exfiltration. Recommend engaging outside counsel promptly to scope notification duties, and engaging forensic review of access logs covering at minimum prior 90 days to establish whether exploitation signatures appear. Will brief executive team on remediation completion and forensic findings within ten business days.

@flint v0 hybrid
G: diagnose_checkout_pool_starvation
C: pool_size("20") ∧ acquire_timeout("5000ms") ∧ reaper_scan("~8M rows") ∧ reaper_period("~23min") ∧ recovery_on_reap_end ∧ incidents("22:04,22:27,22:50") ∧ error_rate("0.97") ∧ p99_spike("340ms->5047ms")
P: correlate("pool_timeout_vs_reaper_start") ∧ observe("timeouts_precede_reaper_log_by_ms") ∧ infer("reaper_holds_conns_during_8m_row_scan") ∧ note("rollback_is_downstream_of_acquire_fail") ∧ note("recovery_exactly_when_reap_finishes") ∧ identify("periodic_saturation_every_23min")
V: timing_alignment("reap_start~pool_exhaust") ∧ recovery_coincides_with_reap_done ∧ three_incidents_same_shape ∧ active_eq_pool_size_with_waiting_queue
A: chunk_reaper_deletes("batch+limit") ∧ add_index("sessions.expires_at") ∧ isolate_pool("scheduler_separate_datasource") ∧ reduce_reaper_tx_scope("no_long_tx") ∧ run_offpeak_or_replica("move_load") ∧ raise_pool_or_shorten_acquire_backoff

[AUDIT]
Periodic expired-session reaper scans ~8M rows and monopolizes the 20-conn pool during its ~4s run; checkout threads queue behind it, hit the 5s acquire timeout, and throw RollbackException because no tx ever started. Pattern repeats every ~23 min (22:04, 22:27, 22:50) and recovers the instant the reaper finishes. Fix: chunked deletes, index on expires_at, and a separate datasource/pool for the scheduler.

input.audit: Periodic expired-session reaper scans ~8M rows and monopolizes the 20-conn pool during its ~4s run; checkout threads queue behind it, hit the 5s acquire timeout, and throw RollbackException because no tx ever started. Pattern repeats every ~23 min (22:04, 22:27, 22:50) and recovers the instant the reaper finishes. Fix: chunked deletes, index on expires_at, and a separate datasource/pool for the scheduler.

Periodic expired-session reaper scans ~8M rows and monopolizes the 20-conn pool during its ~4s run; checkout threads queue behind it, hit the 5s acquire timeout, and throw RollbackException because no tx ever started. Pattern repeats every ~23 min (22:04, 22:27, 22:50) and recovers the instant the reaper finishes. Fix: chunked deletes, index on expires_at, and a separate datasource/pool for the scheduler.

content:
Reaper and checkout share same connection pool.

Reaper opens long transaction to scan ~8M-row sessions table, grabs N connections, holds them for ~4s.

Scan likely unindexed on expires_at, so full table scan under single tx.

During scan, checkout requests cannot acquire conn, queue grows to 47-61 waiters, hit 5000ms timeout.

RollbackException follows because handler tries to commit tx that never started (acquire failed mid-flow).

Pool recovers instant reaper commits and releases conns (waiting=0, active=2 at 22:04:22.891).

Period ~23min matches fixed scheduler cadence, not load-driven — explains deterministic recurrence at 22:04, 22:27, 22:50.

Row count growing (~140k reaped each cycle) suggests reaper barely keeps up, scan window may lengthen until pool never drains.

Root cause: scheduler and request path contend on shared bounded pool while reaper runs table-sized scan inside one transaction.

@flint v0 hybrid
G: prevent_pool_starvation_keep_reaper
C: shared_pool("20") ∧ reaper_must_run ∧ session_growth_real ∧ no_delay_allowed ∧ tx_scope_is_culprit ∧ missing_index_on_expires_at
P: isolate_reaper_datasource("small_dedicated_pool") ∧ add_index("CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_sessions_expires_at ON sessions(expires_at)") ∧ chunked_delete_loop("DELETE ... WHERE expires_at < now() LIMIT 5000") ∧ commit_per_batch("no_long_tx") ∧ sleep_between_batches("50ms yield") ∧ cap_reaper_pool("max=2")
V: checkout_active_stays_below_pool_max_during_reap ∧ reaper_still_drains_session_backlog_per_cycle ∧ p99_checkout_stays_near_baseline ∧ no_acquire_timeouts_in_next_reap_windows ∧ reap_duration_may_grow_but_no_request_impact
A: config("spring.datasource.checkout.maximum-pool-size=20") ∧ config("spring.datasource.reaper.maximum-pool-size=2") ∧ code("@Scheduled loop: while(rows=jdbc.update(DELETE FROM sessions WHERE expires_at<? LIMIT 5000, now()))>0 { Thread.sleep(50); }") ∧ migration("CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_sessions_expires_at") ∧ remove("@Transactional on reaper method")

[AUDIT]
Minimal fix: give reaper its own 2-connection datasource so it cannot starve the 20-conn checkout pool, add index on sessions.expires_at so deletes are O(log n) not O(8M), and replace the single long transaction with a chunked DELETE ... LIMIT 5000 loop that commits each batch. Reaper keeps running every cycle, session growth still drained, but checkout pool is structurally insulated.

P: add_index("CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_sessions_expires_at ON sessions(expires_at)")
A: code("@Scheduled loop: while(rows=jdbc.update(DELETE FROM sessions WHERE expires_at<? LIMIT 5000, now()))>0 { Thread.sleep(50); }")

content:
On evening of incident, checkout endpoint experienced three windows of degraded availability between 22:04 and 22:50. During each window, roughly 97% of checkout requests failed or timed out for about 4-5 seconds before service returned to normal latency. Total customer-impacting time spanned 23 minutes across three recurrences, though any single checkout attempt saw at most a few seconds of failure before retry would have succeeded.

Impact limited to /api/checkout. Browsing, cart updates, account pages, and payment callbacks continued to operate normally. Orders already in flight when windows began were either completed on retry or safely rolled back; no duplicate charges, no lost orders, no data corruption observed in post-incident reconciliation.

Root cause traces to interaction between two internal systems that shared same database connection pool. Background job responsible for cleaning up expired user sessions had grown to scan roughly eight million rows per run. While that scan executed, it held enough database connections that incoming checkout requests could not acquire one, and after five seconds of waiting they failed. Once cleanup finished (about four seconds later), pool drained and checkout recovered immediately. Because cleanup ran on fixed schedule, pattern repeated every twenty-three minutes until mitigation deployed.

Fix has three parts, now rolled out. Cleanup job given its own small, isolated connection pool so it can never again contend with customer traffic. Added database index so cleanup work is proportional to number of actually-expired sessions rather than full table size. Cleanup rewritten to process sessions in small batches with commits between them, so it never holds connections for long. Combined effect: job still runs on same cadence, session growth still handled, checkout pool structurally protected.

We regret disruption and time it cost customers mid-checkout. Incident surfaced architectural assumption (shared pool between scheduled work and request path) that had been safe at smaller data volumes and silently became unsafe as session table grew. We are auditing other scheduled jobs for same pattern and adding pool-saturation alerts that would have paged us minutes earlier. If your checkout failed and you believe order was affected, please contact support and we will reconcile directly.

Most honest read: this incident was not caused by bad code but by code that quietly outgrew its assumptions. Reaper was written when sessions table held maybe fifty thousand rows and full scan completed in milliseconds. Nobody revisited it as table crossed into millions, because nothing was breaking and no alert fired. Process gap was not "we shipped bug" but "we had no mechanism to re-evaluate old code against new data volumes." Periodic review of scheduled jobs against current table sizes, even lightweight quarterly sweep, would have surfaced this long before 22:04 last night. Same applies to any query whose cost scales with dataset: we treated them as write-once, but they need re-reading as system grows.

Second gap lives in our testing posture. Staging runs on roughly 1% of production data, so reaper finishes in under a hundred milliseconds there and touches pool imperceptibly. Load tests exercise checkout path but do not run alongside scheduled jobs, so contention between them never appears in any pre-prod signal. Closing that gap does not require production-scale staging; it requires deliberate test that runs request traffic while scheduled jobs execute, even against synthetic data sized to stress pool. Had we done that once, starvation pattern would have shown up in CI metrics rather than customer checkout attempts.

Final reflection concerns observability. Pool saturation is textbook leading indicator, and we had metrics for it, but no alert threshold below "already timing out." We noticed reaper cycles on dashboards because error rate spiked, not because waiting-queue depth or active-connections ratio crossed anything. Going forward we want alerts that page on pool pressure before it becomes user-visible failure, plus lightweight cross-referencing of scheduled-job runtimes against request latency so that correlation we eventually spotted by eye would surface automatically. Incident taught us less about databases and more about how silently safe systems drift into unsafe ones when no one is watching specifically for drift.
